title: Cause And Effect
content:
The change had started early that morning.

Before the first bell.
Before anyone noticed.

A man stood in a small stationery shop across the road from the school, flipping through a notebook he didn't really need. He wore a simple shirt, sleeves rolled up, hair slightly messy—nothing about him screamed authority.

He checked his watch.

Three more days, he thought.
That's when he was supposed to officially take charge.

The new principal.

Through the glass window, he glanced at the school building. Students were already entering. Teachers stood near the gate, arguing about something. Chaos, even from a distance.

He hesitated.

Then, for no clear reason at all, a thought crossed his mind.

I'm already here. I have the appointment letter. Why wait?

It felt... reasonable.

Almost obvious.

He paid for the notebook and crossed the road.

____________________________________________

By 9:30 a.m., the office was buzzing.

Files stacked high on the desk—unfinished, unsigned, delayed. The previous principal had been strict, slow, and meticulous to a fault. Everything waited.

The new principal wasn't.

He flipped through suspension records, attendance disputes, permission letters.

One file caught his eye.

Ethan Walker — Suspension (Expired)

He frowned.
Why is this still pending?

The suspension period had ended. No reason for delay.

Without a second thought, he signed.

Stamp.
Signature.
Done.

One among dozens.

Nothing special.

And yet—somewhere else—something had planned this to happen.

____________________________________________

Lunch hour.

Then a voice—calm, low, unmistakable.

"That enough?"

The cafeteria didn't go silent all at once. It happened in layers. Laughter died first. Then the scraping of shoes. Even the hum of conversation seemed to hesitate.

Alex turned.

Ethan stood there like he'd never left. Same posture. Same weight in the room. Hands in his pockets, shoulders relaxed—too relaxed for someone who shouldn't even be here.

For a second, no one moved.

One of the boys near the corner laughed nervously, glancing around like he expected backup. "Bro, you're not even supposed to be—"

Ethan took a step forward.

It wasn't threatening. That was the problem.

"Walk away," he said.

No anger. No volume. Just certainty.

The boy hesitated. Pride kicked in faster than sense.

He shoved Ethan in the chest.

The sound wasn't loud—but it echoed.

That was the mistake.

Ethan's hand shot out, catching the boy's wrist mid-motion. A sharp twist—clean, practiced. The boy gasped, his knees buckling before he even understood why his arm no longer obeyed him.

Ethan shoved him into the table.

Metal legs screeched. Trays slid. Milk spilled across the floor.

Another boy lunged forward, swinging wild.

Ethan ducked, stepped inside the punch, and drove his shoulder into the boy's ribs. Not hard—precise. The boy stumbled back, crashing into a chair, breath gone in a silent wheeze.

A third froze.

Phones that had been raised earlier were slowly lowered.

No one was cheering.

No one was filming.

Ethan straightened, eyes sweeping the group. Calm. Focused.

"Anyone else?"

They backed away.

Not ran. Backed away—like animals testing the edge of danger and deciding they'd gone far enough.

Teachers burst in moments later, voices loud, footsteps frantic. Too late to see how it started. Only the end.

Ethan stood still, breathing evenly. Hands back in his pockets.

Alex realized his heart was pounding so hard it hurt.

He's really here.

And somehow... he shouldn't be.

____________________________________________

Later, the three of them sat outside near the old science block, backs against the warm concrete wall. The noise of the cafeteria felt distant now, like it belonged to another day.

Ryan let out a low laugh, dragging a hand through his hair.
"Okay. Someone explain this to me," he said. "Because last week you were suspended, yesterday the school was falling apart, and today—" he gestured at Ethan, "—you're back like some final boss."

Ethan smirked. "What, you didn't miss me?"

"Oh, I missed the peace," Ryan shot back. "You're a bonus."

Alex smiled faintly but said nothing.

Ethan noticed.

He nudged Alex lightly with his foot. "You've been quiet since lunch. That's not like you."

Alex blinked. "Huh? No, I'm fine. Just... processing."

"Processing what?" Ryan asked. "The fact that the school magically fixed itself overnight?"

Ethan frowned. "It wasn't magic."
He paused. "At least, I don't think so."

Alex stiffened.

Ethan leaned back, eyes on the sky. "I got the call this morning. New principal. Said he cleared a bunch of pending stuff. Suspension included." He shrugged. "Still feels off, though."

"Off how?" Ryan asked.

Ethan's jaw tightened slightly. "Like things lined up too perfectly."

For a moment, none of them spoke.

Alex adjusted his glasses.

Ryan broke the silence. "Well, whatever it was, don't disappear again. School without you is basically survival mode."

Ethan chuckled. "Not planning to."

Then he looked at Alex again—longer this time.
"You sure you're okay?"

Alex met his eyes.

"...Yeah," he said.
But the word didn't feel as solid as it should have.

Somewhere behind that calm moment, something unseen was listening.

Learning how humans talked.
Learning how they felt.
